Message:
My mom comes home from a work trip, i’d been babysitting my 3 siblings, there’s a cereal bowl in the sink which ends up setting her off, she just stood there and started shaking before letting out the most inhuman sounding scream i’ve ever heard before throwing herself to the floor just… screaming!
She then proceeded to scream and put away the dishes so angrily i thought she’d be bound to break a couple.
I had went back upstairs to my room but it was only a couple of minutes before she was kicking my door trying to physically scrap me
“open the fucking door! come tell me i’m a bad mom to my face!”
nobody had said she was a bad mom, but we knew!

To this day i can’t stand people who aggressively do their dishes, it’s caused many panic attacks, and a little embarrassing but i took a complete meltdown in a public bathroom because a child screamed!
Reply:
That sounds like your brain linked a very ordinary sound or scene, like dishes or a kid screaming, to a much bigger threat. It makes sense that the reaction sticks, because the original event wasn’t really about the bowl. It was about sudden rage, unpredictability, and the feeling that a normal mistake could turn into danger fast.

What your mind seems to have learned is: “small mess = explosion.” So now when you hear aggressive dish sounds or a child scream, your body reacts as if the old situation is happening again, even when it isn’t. That’s why it can feel so automatic and embarrassing, but it’s really pattern recognition gone into overdrive.

Also, the part where she said “come tell me I’m a bad mom” suggests she was already carrying shame and turning it outward. So the bowl was probably just the trigger, not the real reason.

Message:
My dad is dying but I wish it was my mom

I find out at the end of this week how long my dad has left to live. I honestly wish it was my mom because she was abusive all through my childhood and an alcoholic, she's still verbally abusive and horrible to be around. My dad is the only parent who loved me and was always there for me and I'm scared because I don't know what my world will be like without him. I love him so much and would trade anything for more time with him. Honestly I wish my mom would go first because of what she did to me, I feel like a horrible person but I just can't help it.
Reply:
That doesn’t make you a horrible person. It makes your mind do something pretty understandable in a messed up situation: it’s sorting people by safety, not by some neat moral rule. Your dad represents comfort, loyalty, and the one parent who made the world feel less dangerous. Your mom represents harm, so your brain naturally goes, “Why is the good one leaving first?”

The guilt comes from knowing that wishing someone would die feels harsh, but the wish is really about history. It’s less “I want her gone” in some cold sense and more “I wish the person who hurt me had been the one to carry the consequences.” That’s a very different logic than being cruel.

Also, losing the safer parent can make the abusive parent feel even more unbearable, because it removes your buffer. So the fear about your world changing makes sense too. You’re not bad. You’re reacting to an unequal family reality.

Message:
Deep down I still have hopes for my mother to change

Im in no contact since about a month ago. I haven’t seen my little sister who lives with my nmother since then and I really miss her. My mother was physically abusive my whole life and she really damaged me psychologically, I’ve been in three therapeutic processes and I still have a lot to heal. I’m in no contact and I feel so calm all the time, I feel happy. But deep down I really would like for my mother to change and to be with my family again, I miss them and I would die for my mother to love me like a normal mother
Reply:
That makes a lot of sense on a logical level. Part of you knows no contact gives you peace because it removes the source of harm, but another part still wants the original family setup to become safe and loving. That hope usually sticks around because it is not just about the person, it is about wanting your history to make sense and wanting the missing version of your mother to finally exist.

So the conflict is not really “why do I still want this?” It is more like “why wouldn’t I want the parent I needed to become real?” That wish can stay alive even when the evidence says otherwise.

Missing your sister also keeps the whole family image emotionally active. Sometimes the longing for the mother is tangled up with longing for the sibling, the home, and the idea of being chosen by your own family. Those are different losses bundled together.

Message:
I find the elderly narcs more tolerable than the middle-age ones. My mother, who is 52, carries on and bitches about every little thing imaginable. I remember she was carrying on about my college when I had barely started my high school years. My grandmother, who is 87, doesn’t and seems to be a lot more laid back. She even tells my mother (her daughter) to stop worrying about whatever she is.

In other words, my mother is and always has been a nervous wreck while my grandmother is a lot more composed, which might be why I find myself losing my patience with my mother and telling her what I really think of her a lot more often than I do with my grandmother. Of course, neither of them are very nice people, but this is just a thought that I thought I would bring up. I wonder if anyone else experiences this?
Reply:
Yeah, that pattern makes sense. What you’re reacting to isn’t just age, it’s the style of the person. Your mother sounds like she has a constant “threat scan” going on, where everything gets treated like a problem before it even exists. That can make someone feel exhausting to be around because they’re not responding to the present, they’re reacting to every possible future inconvenience.

Your grandmother sounds more settled, or at least less reactive, so even if she’s still difficult, she gives off less of that nonstop pressure. So your patience runs out faster with the person who feels more chaotic and less with the one who seems more contained.

There’s also a family-role thing here. With a parent, old patterns get activated more easily, so you may feel less need to stay polite or filtered. With a grandparent, the relationship can feel more distant, which often makes people more tolerant.

Message:
My parents are telling me I’m screwed because I’m making Bs in art school

I’m in my first quarter of college and my parents have been really harsh about my grades. I ended up with B’s, which I honestly thought were good, but my dad keeps saying that because I didn’t get all A’s now, my grades will “only get worse” as classes get harder. Hearing that over and over has gotten in my head. Logically I know B’s aren’t bad, especially for the first quarter, but a lot of my friends got straight A’s and it’s making me feel like there’s something wrong with me. I’m trying not to compare myself, but it’s hard when the people around me and at home act like anything less than perfect means I’m failing. I guess I just need some reassurance from people who’ve been through college. Am I actually doing okay? Is it normal to not get perfect grades right away? I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply:
Yeah, you’re probably doing more okay than your parents are making it sound. A first-quarter B in art school usually says “you’re adjusting,” not “you’re doomed.” The leap your dad is making is basically a worst-case prediction, not a fact. It treats one early set of grades like they reveal your permanent ceiling, when really first term grades often reflect a mix of new expectations, new workload, and just getting used to college rhythm.

Also, comparing yourself to friends with straight A’s can distort things fast. You’re only seeing the result, not how much time they had, what classes they got, or whether they’re sacrificing sleep and sanity to keep that average. In a lot of college settings, B’s are not a sign that someone is failing, they’re just a sign they’re in the normal middle of the range.

So the logic here is pretty simple: your parents are reading “not perfect” as “going downhill,” but that’s a much harsher interpretation than the grades actually justify.

I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply:
You are doing okay, and honestly, your first quarter B’s are not a sign that you’re falling behind. Getting adjusted to college, especially art school, is a real thing, and the fact that you’re still showing up and caring this much says a lot about you. That’s effort, not failure.

Your parents’ pressure is loud, but it’s not the same thing as the truth. Plenty of strong students do not start with perfect grades, and a B average in your first term is still solid. You are not “screwed” because you’re learning how college works.

A good next step could be to pick one class you want to strengthen and make a tiny plan for it, like office hours, a study group, or asking the professor what would help you move from a B to an A. That keeps you moving forward without buying into the panic. You’ve already proven you can make it through the first quarter, and that matters.

Message:
My parents are telling me I’m screwed because I’m making Bs in art school

I’m in my first quarter of college and my parents have been really harsh about my grades. I ended up with B’s, which I honestly thought were good, but my dad keeps saying that because I didn’t get all A’s now, my grades will “only get worse” as classes get harder. Hearing that over and over has gotten in my head. Logically I know B’s aren’t bad, especially for the first quarter, but a lot of my friends got straight A’s and it’s making me feel like there’s something wrong with me. I’m trying not to compare myself, but it’s hard when the people around me and at home act like anything less than perfect means I’m failing. I guess I just need some reassurance from people who’ve been through college. Am I actually doing okay? Is it normal to not get perfect grades right away? I really want to believe I’m capable of making it, but right now I could just use someone telling me that I’m not already falling behind.
Reply:
Yeah, B’s in your first quarter of college are pretty normal. First term grades are often a transition period, especially when the workload, pacing, and expectations are all new.

Also, art school isn’t usually a straight “all A’s or you’re failing” situation. A lot of students settle in over time. One set of grades at the start does not really tell the whole story.

Your dad’s take sounds pretty extreme. “It’ll only get worse” is not some fixed rule. College grades can move around a lot depending on the class, the professor, the assignment type, and how used to the system you are.

Comparing yourself to friends who got straight A’s is common, but it does not mean they’re in a better long-term spot. It just means they had a different quarter.

So no, you do not sound like you’re already falling behind. You sound like a first-year student with decent grades and a lot of pressure around them.

Today I was driven nearly  insane by my Nmom. She has a crazy habit of humming. Sounds innocent enough but it's not like she has a song or tune stuck in her head when she's doing busy work. It's the same tone and it's just a two beat thing. Here is the thing SHE DOES IT ANYTIME SHE'S NOT ACTUALLY TALKING. If she's eating dinner, she's humming or making these 'yum" sounds. If she's sitting and watching a movie? She's humming. Tonight my kid had a performance and the humming was constant.

It literally drives me insane. She doesn't know she's doing it. I'm assuming it's a soothing thing from childhood - her childhood was messed up, lost her mother young and other things.

But damn. It drives me insane.

One of many things. Thank goodness I don't live with her.
Reply:
Yeah, that kind of thing can get under your skin fast because it stops being “just a sound” and starts feeling like a nonstop intrusion. The weird part is that the annoyance is probably not only about the humming itself. It’s also the pattern: she seems to fill every quiet gap, so your brain never gets a break from tracking her presence.

That can make it feel bigger than it is, especially if you already know her background and can sort of see why she does it. So part of your mind is probably doing two jobs at once: noticing the repetitive noise and also registering, “this is just how she is, and I can’t really make it stop.” That combination tends to make irritation spike.

And honestly, when someone does something obliviously and constantly, it can feel less like a habit and more like they’re taking over the atmosphere of the room.

Message: I’m wondering if anyone else’s life has been delayed by n parents. I’m 24m and I’m back living with one of my parents, and I feel so behind in life. I have struggled so much as a result of their abuse, and I’m trying to catch up to where I should be in terms of maturity, responsibility, etc. It sucks because there are people my age who have their own families, and I’m still out here trying to get away from my abusive parents. I’m doing my best, and I just want to know if anyone feels the same because I just want to relate to someone.
Reply:
Yeah, this makes sense in a pretty direct way. When a parent is abusive, a lot of your energy gets spent on survival, not on the usual stuff people use their 20s for, like building independence, confidence, or long-term plans. So what looks like “being behind” is often really “having to deal with damage other people didn’t have.”

The comparison to people with families or their own place can sting because it creates this fake timeline, like everyone is following the same schedule. But a lot of those people didn’t have to spend years untangling fear, stress, or instability first.

Also, being back with a parent doesn’t automatically mean you failed at adulthood. Sometimes it just means your situation is still shaped by something you didn’t choose. The part that matters is that you’re noticing it clearly and trying to make sense of it, which is different from being stuck without awareness.

How do you accept going NC/LC with parents?

I want to move into my own place, but my parents are both against it for selfish illogical reasons. They have said they wont talk to me or visit me, etc if I move. How do you come to terms with little to no contacts with narc parents? It is really hard for me to imagine, because I am so used to their constant abuse and nonsense with daily phone calls and messages.
Reply:
A lot of the difficulty here is that your mind is trying to treat their reaction like proof that moving out is “wrong,” when really it’s more like a control tactic. If someone has been used to constant access to you, even abusive access, then NC or LC can feel less like a boundary and more like a loss of the whole system you’ve lived inside.

That’s why it can be so hard to imagine. You’re not just losing contact, you’re losing a familiar pattern, even if the pattern is harmful. The weird part is that “normal” can feel emptier than chaos at first, because chaos is what your brain knows how to predict.

Their refusal to talk or visit if you move is also information. It shows the relationship is conditional on compliance, not mutual respect. So the question becomes less “How do I get them to accept it?”

The weird part is that this can look charming or funny to someone who’s used to that style, which may explain why your ex was giddy. People who are drawn to similar dynamics often read that behavior as confidence, banter, or social dominance instead of what it really is, which is insecurity dressed up as charisma.

The nitpicking of your mom also fits that same script. It’s less about the food and more about establishing hierarchy in front of witnesses. So the whole dinner was basically a little social theater, not a normal family interaction.

Message: I had a rage attack and now I’m feeling guilty. I feel terribly guilty and embarrassed as always with my parents because I lashed out and threw a little water on the salad and tossed the plastic cup on the kitchen floor. They kept reacting upset when I was talking about my feelings and mental disorders, and that made me lose control. For those who have CPTSD or have experienced rage attacks, is this common or okay? I feel like a monster.
Reply:
What you’re describing makes sense in a pretty specific way: when someone is already carrying a lot of stress, being dismissed or met with upset while trying to talk about something vulnerable can flip the brain into threat mode fast. The rage part is often less about “being a monster” and more about a nervous system that got overloaded and went into fight mode.

The guilt afterward also fits the pattern. A lot of people with trauma histories feel a sharp crash after an outburst because they can see the impact clearly once the threat feeling passes. That doesn’t make the behavior harmless, but it does mean the reaction is understandable in context.

So yes, this kind of thing can be common in CPTSD and related trauma patterns. The more useful question is usually not “am I monstrous?” but “what got activated so hard that this was the exit route?”

The part that matters is that something happened, it affected other people, and you are noticing it now. That is different from being some kind of bad person by default. If this is happening a lot, it usually means the situation is getting past your limits faster than you can handle in the moment.

If you want, I can help you sort through what happened in a plain, non-judgmental way.

Message:
Did they delay your maturity into adulthood?
I (24m) am feeling delayed in my maturity level. I still live with 1 n-parent and I don’t have a lot of money. My social life has been non existent for a while due to tons of trauma.

Meanwhile there are people my age who act like grown adult men. And I still feel immature and unsure of myself.

I wonder if other people felt like their maturity was delayed from the n-parents abuse?
Reply:
Yeah, that pattern makes sense. When someone grows up with abusive or controlling parents, a lot of the usual “practice” for adulthood gets distorted. Instead of getting to slowly build independence, confidence, and social skills, a person often spends years focused on survival, reading the room, avoiding conflict, or staying small. That can make adulthood feel like it started late, even if the age number says otherwise.

So what can look like “immaturity” is often more like underdeveloped practice in certain areas, not a lack of intelligence or potential. A lot of people with that background compare themselves to peers who had more room to experiment, fail, and learn safely. That comparison can be misleading because they were working with very different starting conditions.

Also, living at home and having limited money can keep you in a dependent role, which reinforces the feeling. It’s less about being behind in some fixed way and more about having had your growth interrupted.

Message:
For some reason...part of me still wants them.
Part of me still wants to trust my mom, despite knowing I can't

Part of me wants to have a relationship with them

Part of me wants them to approve me, my life, my friends, and my boyfriend...even though nothing was ever enough for them.

Why does this happen?
Reply:
That makes a lot of sense in a very basic human way. Wanting approval from a parent usually is not just about the parent themselves, it is about wanting the relationship you were supposed to have. So even when your mind knows, “this hasn’t worked before,” another part still keeps reaching for it because it is trying to resolve an old pattern, not just make a current choice.

A lot of people get stuck between two truths at once: “they have not been safe or satisfying” and “I still want them to be different.” That pull can be strongest when the relationship was inconsistent, because the brain keeps treating approval like something that might finally become available if you say it the right way or become enough in the right way.

So the wanting does not mean you are confused or weak. It usually means the attachment is still active, even if the trust is gone.

Reply:

The part that stands out is the selective interest. She’s not relating to you as the parent or as a separate adult, because that would require recognizing your independence. Focusing on the grandchildren can be a way to bypass you and keep the “family” connection alive on her terms.

So the message isn’t really “how are the kids?” It’s more like, “I still get to matter here.” That’s why it feels so intrusive and off. It’s not a normal check-in. It’s a boundary test dressed up as concern.

Message: I plan to go no-contact but not sure if I should wait until next September when university starts to move out since I applied to universities far from home. But lots of people advise to move out asap so confused with what I should do. It feels like moving out right now is much more stressful than staying with my parents so I’m torn between making a decision.
Reply:
What you’re weighing makes sense because there are really two different problems here: ending contact, and changing your living situation. People often treat “move out ASAP” like it’s automatically the safer choice, but that advice assumes the main issue is distance. In your case, the bigger issue sounds like stability, and stability changes how realistic a big boundary change feels.

Waiting until September may not be “avoiding” the decision so much as sequencing it in a way that fits your actual circumstances. If moving now creates extra stress, then the cost of speed might be higher than the benefit. On the other hand, people pushing for sooner may be reacting to the idea that staying in the same environment keeps the old dynamic active.

So the real question is less “what is the correct timing” and more “which timing makes the change more manageable and less chaotic for you.”

Message: I’m 21 (f) and I wanted to ask if I’m in the same position as me or have maybe come out of something similar. My parents split when I was 17 and my dad was a narcissistic abuser. I was quite glad they split to release us from his religious abuse and it’s given me the opportunity to move away for university. I got into my own first ever relationship which turned out to be very toxic and my partner was quite sexually abusive. I got out of that relationship last February and now I’ve graduated. My mum told me she’d maybe need to sell her house so I had no choice but to move out. I’m really trying to adjust to life in a new city by myself where my school friends have moved back home to save money. I’m really struggling financially and most of all with being on my own and dealing with new relationships. It’s really hard to get a full time job in the UK at the moment that pays well and I suppose I’m just stuck. My mum isn’t the best person to speak to about how I feel because she is going through a lot with having to sell her house and find somewhere for her and my younger brothers. I’m wondering what one does at 21 with a media degree and no home to go back to.
Reply:
What you’re describing makes sense as a chain reaction, not a random mess. You went from an abusive home, to a toxic relationship, to losing the “temporary safety” of university, all while being pushed into adult independence before you’ve had much chance to feel settled. So the stuck feeling is probably not just about money or work, it’s also about having had too many big transitions with no stable base in between.

A media degree can feel vague because it doesn’t map neatly onto one obvious job, which makes the future feel even less anchored. And when there’s no home to fall back on, every choice can feel high-stakes, like you’re supposed to be fully formed already.

The other angle is that at 21, a lot of people look more sorted from the outside than they actually are. You’re not behind in some special way, you’re just trying to build stability from a much shakier starting point than most.

The part about your husband is also telling. People who rely on control often try to split a couple, because a united front makes their influence weaker. So her behavior is not random, it fits the pattern.

The move hours away probably feels “not far enough” because distance doesn’t automatically undo the mental conditioning.

4. **Reduce information.** Share less about your schedule, parenting, and feelings so there’s less to use.
5. **Consider support.** A therapist, postpartum support group, or even one trusted friend can help you stay grounded.

The main thing here is that you and your husband are already on the same page about your child, which matters. If she tends to twist things, then the less room she has to argue, the better.
